summary: Jeff Atwood argues that over-reliance on the mouse is holding back computer productivity, and advocates for deliberately learning keyboard shortcuts by forcing yourself to stop reaching for the mouse. He acknowledges mice have their place and remembers the pre-mouse era, but observes most users have swung too far toward mouse dependency. He recommends learning at least one new keyboard shortcut per day, noting the initial awkwardness pays dividends in long-term efficiency. While conceding that web navigation remains stubbornly mouse-centric, he maintains that mastering both input methods leads to optimal computing.
key_points: Keyboard shortcuts are almost always more efficient than mouse navigation, but people won't learn them while relying on the mouse as a crutch
The recommended approach is forcing yourself to learn at least one new keyboard shortcut per day
Applications should do more to make keyboard shortcuts discoverable, like Office 2007's approach of lighting up available shortcuts when accelerator keys are pressed
HTML and web navigation remain frustratingly mouse-centric, making keyboard-only browsing impractical for most users
The goal isn't eliminating the mouse entirely, but achieving balanced proficiency with both input methods
money_quotes: One of the quickest ways to increase your productivity on the computer is to go commando: stop using the mouse.
Stop for a moment, resist taking the easy way out with the mouse, and force yourself to learn at least one new keyboard shortcut per day.
Most people I've observed using the computer these days rely almost exclusively on the mouse, to the detriment of their overall computer experience.
It's a shame more applications don't go out of their way to make keyboard shortcuts more discoverable.
I guarantee whatever you're trying to do is possible – and ultimately quicker – if you persist with the keyboard.
key_insight: Deliberate short-term discomfort in learning keyboard shortcuts yields lasting productivity gains, but you must actively resist the mouse to force the learning.
